docs.text: Iterators and Functional Data Processing let x = 5; println!("{}", x * 2); 10 Transforming Data with map() The map function is one of the most commonly used transformation tools in Rust. It applies a function to each value in a container or wrapper type and returns a new value with the transformation applied. In Rust, map appears in several places, most commonly: Iterators ( Iterator::map) Option (Option::map) Result (Result::map) use std::collections::HashMap; /// A closed genomic interval: both `start` and `end` are included. /// In bioinformatics this is common, so `100..100` means one base. #[derive(Clone, Debug, PartialEq, Eq)] pub struct Interval { pub start: u64, pub end: u64, } impl Interval { pub fn new(start: u64, end: u64) -> Self { Self { start, end } } pub fn len(&self) -> u64 { // Genomic intervals here are inclusive on both ends. // Example: 10..12 contains 3 bases: 10, 11, and 12. self.end - self.start + 1 } } let mut intervals: Vec<Interval> = vec![ Interval::new(10, 12), Interval::new(20, 25), ]; println!("{:?}", intervals); println!("Length of first interval: {}", intervals[0].len()); println!("Length of second interval: {}", intervals[1].len()); [Interval { start: 10, end: 12 }, Interval { start: 20, end: 25 }] Length of first interval: 3 Length of second interval: 6 // append new intervals intervals.push(Interval::new(20, 35)); println!("{:?}", intervals); println!("The number of intervals: {}", intervals.len()); [Interval { start: 10, end: 12 }, Interval { start: 20, end: 25 }, Interval { start: 20, end: 35 }] The number of intervals: 3
docs.title: Iterators and Functional Data Processing

docs.text: The map function is one of the most commonly used transformation tools in Rust. It applies a function to each value in a container or wrapper type and returns a new value with the transformation applied. In Rust, map appears in several places, most commonly: Iterators ( Iterator::map) Option (Option::map) Result (Result::map) use std::collections::HashMap; /// A closed genomic interval: both `start` and `end` are included. /// In bioinformatics this is common, so `100..100` means one base. #[derive(Clone, Debug, PartialEq, Eq)] pub struct Interval { pub start: u64, pub end: u64, } impl Interval { pub fn new(start: u64, end: u64) -> Self { Self { start, end } } pub fn len(&self) -> u64 { // Genomic intervals here are inclusive on both ends. // Example: 10..12 contains 3 bases: 10, 11, and 12. self.end - self.start + 1 } } let mut intervals: Vec<Interval> = vec![ Interval::new(10, 12), Interval::new(20, 25), ]; println!("{:?}", intervals); println!("Length of first interval: {}", intervals[0].len()); println!("Length of second interval: {}", intervals[1].len()); [Interval { start: 10, end: 12 }, Interval { start: 20, end: 25 }] Length of first interval: 3 Length of second interval: 6 // append new intervals intervals.push(Interval::new(20, 35)); println!("{:?}", intervals); println!("The number of intervals: {}", intervals.len()); [Interval { start: 10, end: 12 }, Interval { start: 20, end: 25 }, Interval { start: 20, end: 35 }] The number of intervals: 3

docs.text: Core Syntax & Types Why Syntax Matters in Bioinformatics In bioinformatics, we routinely process gigabyte–terabyte scale data (FASTQ, BAM, VCF). Small inefficiencies in syntax or type usage can translate into hours of runtime or gigabytes of wasted memory. Rust’s syntax is not just aesthetic—it enforces: Memory safety (no silent bugs) Explicit data handling High performance without a garbage collector This chapter builds the foundation for writing correct and fast genomic pipelines. Variables and Immutability Rust variables are immutable by default. let x = 10; // x = 20; // error If you run the Rust code below: let x = 10; x = 20; // error You will get the error message shown below: [E0384] Error: cannot assign twice to immutable variable `x` ╭─[ command_2:1:1 ] │ 1 │ let x = 10; │ ┬ │ ╰── first assignment to `x` │ │ │ ╰── help: consider making this binding mutable: `mut ` 2 │ x = 20; // error │ ───┬── │ ╰──── cannot assign twice to immutable variable │ │ Note: You can change an existing variable to mutable like: `let mut x = x;` ───╯ [unused_assignments] Error: value assigned to `x` is never read ╭─[ command_2:1:1 ] │ 1 │ let x = 10; │ ─┬ │ ╰── warning: value assigned to `x` is never read ───╯ Mutable variables let mut count = 0; count += 1; println!("count: {}", count); count: 1 Use mut only when necessary—this reduces bugs and improves reasoning. Shadowing (Rebinding Variables) let x = 5; let x = x + 1; let x = x * 2; println!("x: {}", x); x: 12 Unlike mutation, shadowing creates a new variable. let seq = "acgt"; let seq = seq.to_uppercase(); println!("seq: {}", seq); seq: ACGT You transform data without mutating the original reference. Scalar Types Integers let x: i32 = 10; let y: u64 = 100; Use cases: u64 → genome positions i32 → score differences Floating point let gc_ratio: f64 = 0.42; Boolean let is_mapped = true; let is_cell = false; println!("is_mapped: {}", is_mapped); println!("is_cell: {}", is_cell); is_mapped: true is_cell: false Character
docs.title: Core syntax and types

docs.text: Read and Write files in Rust use std::fs::File; use std::io::{self, BufRead}; use std::path::Path; fn main() -> io::Result<()> { let file = File::open("../data/book/ref.fa")?; let reader = io::BufReader::new(file); for line in reader.lines() { let line = line?; println!("{}", line); } Ok(()) } main() >chr1 ATCGGTCATCGATCGATGCTAGCTAGCTAGCTAGCTACG >chr2 GGTCGATGCTAGCTAGCTAGCTAGCTAGCTAGCT Ok(())
docs.title: Read QC and alignment

docs.text: use std::fs::File; use std::io::{self, BufRead}; use std::path::Path; fn main() -> io::Result<()> { let file = File::open("../data/book/ref.fa")?; let reader = io::BufReader::new(file); for line in reader.lines() { let line = line?; println!("{}", line); } Ok(()) } main() >chr1 ATCGGTCATCGATCGATGCTAGCTAGCTAGCTAGCTACG >chr2 GGTCGATGCTAGCTAGCTAGCTAGCTAGCTAGCT Ok(())
docs.title: Read and Write files in Rust

docs.text: Iterators and Functional Data Processing let x = 5; println!("{}", x * 2); 10 Transforming Data with map() The map function is one of the most commonly used transformation tools in Rust. It applies a function to each value in a container or wrapper type and returns a new value with the transformation applied. In Rust, map appears in several places, most commonly: Iterators ( Iterator::map) Option (Option::map) Result (Result::map) let numbers = vec![1, 2, 3, 4]; let doubled: Vec<i32> = numbers .iter() .map(|x| x * 2) .collect(); println!("Original: {:?}", numbers); println!("After doubling: {:?}", doubled); Original: [1, 2, 3, 4] After doubling: [2, 4, 6, 8]
docs.title: Iterators and Functional Data Processing

docs.text: Generics functions and structs use std::fmt::Display; use std::fmt::Debug; fn highest_expr<T: PartialOrd + Copy + Display + Debug> (list: &[T]) -> T { let mut highest = list[0]; for &item in list.iter() { println!("item: {}", item); println!("debug format: {:?}", item); // debug format (also 3 here) println!("display: {}", &item); println!("pointer: {:p}", &item); // prints memory address if item > highest { highest = item; } } highest } let numbers = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9]; let highest_number = highest_expr(&numbers); println!("The highest number is: {}", highest_number); let expr_in_float = [3.0, 1.0, 4.0, 1.0, 5.0, 9.0]; let highest_number = highest_expr(&expr_in_float); println!("The highest number is: {}", highest_number); item: 3 debug format: 3 display: 3 pointer: 0x7ffc32f4be2c item: 1 debug format: 1 display: 1 pointer: 0x7ffc32f4be2c item: 4 debug format: 4 display: 4 pointer: 0x7ffc32f4be2c item: 1 debug format: 1 display: 1 pointer: 0x7ffc32f4be2c item: 5 debug format: 5 display: 5 pointer: 0x7ffc32f4be2c item: 9 debug format: 9 display: 9 pointer: 0x7ffc32f4be2c The highest number is: 9 item: 3 debug format: 3.0 display: 3 pointer: 0x7ffc32f4be20 item: 1 debug format: 1.0 display: 1 pointer: 0x7ffc32f4be20 item: 4 debug format: 4.0 display: 4 pointer: 0x7ffc32f4be20 item: 1 debug format: 1.0 display: 1 pointer: 0x7ffc32f4be20 item: 5 debug format: 5.0 display: 5 pointer: 0x7ffc32f4be20 item: 9 debug format: 9.0 display: 9 pointer: 0x7ffc32f4be20 The highest number is: 9 for &item in list.iter() We are doing pattern matching (destructuring): list.iter() gives &T &item pattern unwraps that reference so inside the loop: item: T
docs.title: Generics functions and structs

docs.text: use std::fmt::Display; use std::fmt::Debug; fn highest_expr<T: PartialOrd + Copy + Display + Debug> (list: &[T]) -> T { let mut highest = list[0]; for &item in list.iter() { println!("item: {}", item); println!("debug format: {:?}", item); // debug format (also 3 here) println!("display: {}", &item); println!("pointer: {:p}", &item); // prints memory address if item > highest { highest = item; } } highest } let numbers = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9]; let highest_number = highest_expr(&numbers); println!("The highest number is: {}", highest_number); let expr_in_float = [3.0, 1.0, 4.0, 1.0, 5.0, 9.0]; let highest_number = highest_expr(&expr_in_float); println!("The highest number is: {}", highest_number); item: 3 debug format: 3 display: 3 pointer: 0x7ffc32f4be2c item: 1 debug format: 1 display: 1 pointer: 0x7ffc32f4be2c item: 4 debug format: 4 display: 4 pointer: 0x7ffc32f4be2c item: 1 debug format: 1 display: 1 pointer: 0x7ffc32f4be2c item: 5 debug format: 5 display: 5 pointer: 0x7ffc32f4be2c item: 9 debug format: 9 display: 9 pointer: 0x7ffc32f4be2c The highest number is: 9 item: 3 debug format: 3.0 display: 3 pointer: 0x7ffc32f4be20 item: 1 debug format: 1.0 display: 1 pointer: 0x7ffc32f4be20 item: 4 debug format: 4.0 display: 4 pointer: 0x7ffc32f4be20 item: 1 debug format: 1.0 display: 1 pointer: 0x7ffc32f4be20 item: 5 debug format: 5.0 display: 5 pointer: 0x7ffc32f4be20 item: 9 debug format: 9.0 display: 9 pointer: 0x7ffc32f4be20 The highest number is: 9 for &item in list.iter() We are doing pattern matching (destructuring): list.iter() gives &T &item pattern unwraps that reference so inside the loop: item: T
docs.title: Generics functions and structs

docs.text: Iterators and Functional Data Processing let x = 5; println!("{}", x * 2); 10 Transforming Data with map() The map function is one of the most commonly used transformation tools in Rust. It applies a function to each value in a container or wrapper type and returns a new value with the transformation applied. In Rust, map appears in several places, most commonly: Iterators ( Iterator::map) Option (Option::map) Result (Result::map) let numbers = vec![1, 2, 3, 4]; let doubled: Vec<i32> = numbers .iter() .map(|x| x * 2) .collect(); println!("Original: {:?}", numbers); println!("After doubling: {:?}", doubled); Original: [1, 2, 3, 4] After doubling: [2, 4, 6, 8]
